scope: Automated search/selection within a pipeline; where it helps and where it just burns compute.
key_concepts: The integration of automated feature engineering into end-to-end AutoML systems.
The concept of the pipeline search space, which includes not just model hyperparameters, but permutations of imputation, scaling, encoding, and feature selection strategies.
The primary value of AutoML: rapidly establishing a solid predictive baseline and handling standard preprocessing without manual trial and error.

The limits of AutoML: why blindly searching massive feature spaces often burns compute without improving generalizability, especially when the underlying problem requires domain expertise or better data quality.

definitions.meaning: Automated Machine Learning; systems designed to automate the iterative tasks of ML development, including preprocessing, feature engineering, model selection, and hyperparameter tuning.
The combinatorial set of all possible sequences of data transformations, feature generation steps, and model algorithms evaluated by an AutoML system.

examples: An AutoML framework automatically generating hundreds of pipelines, testing whether target encoding paired with gradient boosting outperforms one-hot encoding paired with an SVM, while simultaneously pruning generated features.

relations.prerequisites: Ch 2 (pipelines) and Ch 16 s01 (feature space explosion).

exclusions: Avoid explaining hyperparameter search algorithms like Bayesian optimization, TPE, or genetic algorithms. Focus on how AutoML handles feature pipelines.
Do not include heavy code snippets for specific AutoML frameworks (like auto-sklearn or FLAML).
